content.text: Cloud platforms — AWS, Google Cloud Platform, and Azure — provide the managed infrastructure primitives that turn a locally-working AI agent into a production service. Instead of operating bare-metal servers, teams compose managed runtimes, storage, queuing, and observability services while the cloud provider handles capacity, availability, and patching.
is a provider-operated collection of infrastructure services accessed through APIs. For AI agent workloads the relevant tiers are: (1)
(AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps) for stateless agent steps that need millisecond billing; (2)
(ECS Fargate, GKE Autopilot, AKS) for agents that hold in-memory state across calls; and (3) managed
(Amazon Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI Service) that expose foundation models directly without hosting them yourself.

The key distinction from a self-hosted deployment is that the cloud provider manages the control plane: scheduling, auto-scaling, hardware failure recovery, and physical security. You own the data plane — the container image, environment variables, IAM policy, and network topology.

AI agents place unusual demands on infrastructure. They make many outbound API calls (LLM providers, vector stores, external tools), can idle for seconds to minutes between steps, and may spike concurrency sharply when processing a batch of user requests. Cloud platforms are designed to absorb exactly this workload profile: pay-per-invocation pricing eliminates cost during idle periods, scale-to-zero prevents wasted capacity, and global points of presence reduce latency to LLM APIs co-located in the same region.

For teams with no dedicated DevOps staff, managed databases (RDS, Cloud SQL), managed queues (SQS, Cloud Pub/Sub), and managed secret stores (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager) remove entire categories of operational work that would otherwise block shipping.
— serverless functions cost nothing when idle; critical for agents that handle bursty, non-continuous workloads.
— deploy agent replicas to regions co-located with LLM provider endpoints (e.g., Bedrock us-east-1, Vertex AI us-central1) to reduce network RTT.
— Amazon Bedrock, Vertex AI Gemini, and Azure OpenAI Service give access to frontier models via simple HTTPS calls with IAM-based authentication rather than managing API keys.
— auto-scaling groups and HPA (Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) handle sudden traffic spikes without manual intervention.
— CloudWatch, Cloud Monitoring, and Azure Monitor collect logs, metrics, and traces with no extra agents to deploy.
— SOC 2, HIPAA, FedRAMP, and ISO 27001 coverage is inherited from the provider, reducing your own audit burden.
— heavy use of provider-specific services (Bedrock agents, Azure Durable Functions) makes migration expensive. Mitigate by abstracting LLM calls behind a provider-agnostic client.
— Lambda and Cloud Run add 200–2000 ms latency for infrequently invoked functions. Provisioned Concurrency (Lambda) or minimum instances (Cloud Run) eliminates this at additional cost.
— large volumes of data leaving a cloud region (e.g., LLM responses streamed to users in another region) incur egress fees that are easy to overlook during budgeting.
— fine-grained permission models (AWS IAM, GCP IAM, Azure RBAC) require careful design; overly permissive roles are a top cause of cloud security incidents.
— Lambda has a 15-minute maximum; Cloud Run has a 60-minute request timeout. Long-running agent loops must be decomposed into shorter steps via a queue-based handoff or Durable Execution pattern.

The standard cloud deployment flow for an AI agent is: containerize the agent code, push the image to a container registry (ECR, Artifact Registry, ACR), then configure a runtime service to run that image. The runtime pulls the latest image on deploy, distributes traffic across replicas, and restarts unhealthy instances automatically.

Agent invocations arrive via an API gateway (API Gateway, Cloud Endpoints, APIM) or directly from a queue. The agent code runs, makes outbound calls to LLM APIs and tools, and writes results to object storage or a database. Sensitive configuration (API keys, database credentials) is injected at runtime from a secrets service rather than baked into the image.

Auto-scaling works by measuring a scaling metric — typically CPU utilization, request queue depth, or custom metric (token throughput) — and adding or removing replicas to keep the metric near a target value. Serverless platforms abstract this entirely: the control plane manages concurrency per-invocation.
The diagram below shows how the major cloud components connect when running an AI agent service in production.

attrs.alt: Cloud platform architecture for AI agents: developer pushes code through CI/CD into a cloud runtime, backed by managed services, auto-scaler, observability, IAM, object storage, and event bus

content.text: Your team lacks dedicated infrastructure staff and prefers managed services over operational responsibility.
The agent workload is bursty or unpredictable — scale-to-zero billing is materially cheaper than reserved capacity.
You need to deploy quickly and iterate; cloud platforms provide a build-push-deploy cycle measured in minutes.
Compliance requirements (SOC 2, HIPAA) are satisfied through provider certifications rather than building your own controls.
You want co-location benefits with a specific LLM provider (e.g., Bedrock in AWS, Gemini in GCP) to minimise latency and simplify auth.

The implementation below shows deploying a LangChain-based agent to AWS Lambda using a container image. The pattern applies equally to Google Cloud Run and Azure Container Apps by swapping the deployment target.

# Dockerfile
FROM public.ecr.aws/lambda/python:3.12

WORKDIR /var/task

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY agent/ ./agent/

# Lambda handler entrypoint
CMD ["agent.handler.lambda_handler"]

# agent/handler.py
import json
import boto3
from langchain_aws import ChatBedrock
from langchain.agents import create_tool_calling_agent, AgentExecutor
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from .tools import get_tools

# Reuse across warm invocations
llm = ChatBedrock(
    model_id="anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0",
    region_name="us-east-1",
)

tools = get_tools()

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a helpful research assistant."),
    ("human", "{input}"),
    ("placeholder", "{agent_scratchpad}"),
])

agent = create_tool_calling_agent(llm, tools, prompt)
executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=tools, verbose=False)


def lambda_handler(event: dict, context) -> dict:
    body = json.loads(event.get("body", "{}"))
    user_input = body.get("input", "")
    if not user_input:
        return {"statusCode": 400, "body": json.dumps({"error": "input required"})}

    result = executor.invoke({"input": user_input})
    return {
        "statusCode": 200,
        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        "body": json.dumps({"output": result["output"]}),
    }

# Build and push to Artifact Registry
gcloud auth configure-docker us-central1-docker.pkg.dev

docker build -t us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT/agents/my-agent:latest .
docker push us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT/agents/my-agent:latest

# Deploy with Cloud Run
gcloud run deploy my-agent \
  --image us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT/agents/my-agent:latest \
  --region us-central1 \
  --platform managed \
  --memory 2Gi \
  --timeout 3600 \
  --max-instances 20 \
  --min-instances 1 \
  --service-account agent-sa@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com \
  --set-secrets OPENAI_API_KEY=openai-key:latest \
  --no-allow-unauthenticated

# Create a Container Apps environment
az containerapp env create \
  --name agent-env \
  --resource-group rg-agents \
  --location eastus

# Deploy agent
az containerapp create \
  --name my-agent \
  --resource-group rg-agents \
  --environment agent-env \
  --image myacr.azurecr.io/agents/my-agent:latest \
  --target-port 8080 \
  --ingress external \
  --min-replicas 1 \
  --max-replicas 30 \
  --cpu 1 \
  --memory 2Gi \
  --secrets openai-key=keyvaultref:https://myvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/openai-key \
  --env-vars OPENAI_API_KEY=secretref:openai-key

A financial data company runs a document-summarisation agent on AWS Lambda backed by Amazon Bedrock. Each Lambda invocation receives a document ID, fetches the PDF from S3, chunks and embeds it with Amazon Titan, performs RAG against an OpenSearch Serverless collection, calls Claude 3.5 Sonnet through Bedrock for the summary, and writes the result back to S3.

By deploying in the same AWS region as Bedrock (us-east-1), the team eliminated cross-region latency and simplified auth — Lambda's execution role grants Bedrock access via IAM rather than an API key. At peak the system handles 400 concurrent document summarisations with no manual scaling; at quiet periods it costs near-zero. The entire infrastructure is defined in Terraform and deployed via a GitHub Actions pipeline, enabling deterministic re-creation of the environment in a new region for disaster recovery.
— API keys hardcoded in a Dockerfile end up in the image layer history and the ECR repository. Always inject at runtime through Secrets Manager or environment-variable references.
Ignoring cold starts for user-facing paths
— a 2-second cold start is invisible in an async queue but ruins a synchronous chat UX. Use provisioned concurrency or a minimum of one active instance for latency-sensitive flows.

— granting AdministratorAccess to a Lambda function for convenience is a frequent source of privilege-escalation incidents. Scope permissions to the exact S3 bucket, Bedrock model, and secret ARN the function needs.
— Lambda has a hard 15-minute limit. An agent loop that doesn't budget time per LLM call will be terminated mid-run. Set per-call timeouts (e.g., 30 s) and a total budget check before each step.
Underprovisioning memory for LLM client libraries

— LangChain and similar frameworks have significant import overhead. 256 MB is often insufficient; 1–2 GB typically eliminates OOM errors and also reduces cold-start time because Lambda allocates CPU proportionally to memory.
Deploy to the same region as your LLM provider endpoint to minimize network latency and avoid cross-region data transfer costs.
Use container images rather than ZIP deployments for agent workloads — they support images up to 10 GB, making large ML libraries tractable.

Store all mutable configuration (model IDs, prompt templates, tool URLs) in Parameter Store or equivalent, not as environment variables baked at deploy time. This allows hot-patching without a re-deploy.
Apply resource tagging (cost-center, environment, agent-name) from day one. Cloud cost tracking by agent is otherwise impossible in a shared account.
Enable structured JSON logging and propagate a correlation ID through every LLM call and tool call so you can trace a full agent run across CloudWatch or Cloud Logging.
Set a spending alert (AWS Budgets, GCP Budget, Azure Cost Alerts) before you go to production — runaway LLM loops can generate thousands of dollars in minutes.

Cloud platforms scale AI agent workloads by parallelising invocations rather than making individual calls faster. When a queue of 500 documents arrives, 500 Lambda instances can process them concurrently if Lambda's concurrency limit permits — typically the bottleneck shifts to the downstream LLM API's tokens-per-minute rate limit, not the compute layer.
— caps maximum parallelism per function to prevent a single agent from exhausting the account's concurrency limit (default 1000).
SQS batch size and visibility timeout
— tune the queue consumer batch size to match how many tasks the agent can process within the Lambda timeout; set visibility timeout to 2× the expected processing time.
— enabling --cpu-boost on Cloud Run allocates extra CPU during startup, cutting cold start time by 30–50% for Python heavy imports.
— reuse HTTP sessions across invocations by initialising clients at module level (outside the handler function) so they survive warm Lambda instances.
— higher-level IaC abstractions for Lambda that generate CloudFormation; CDK supports TypeScript, Python, Java.
— automatically detect language and build a Cloud Run-compatible image without writing a Dockerfile.
— multi-cloud IaC in general-purpose languages; useful when you deploy across AWS and GCP for redundancy.
GitHub Actions / Cloud Build / Azure Pipelines
— native CI/CD integrations for building agent images and deploying them; OIDC federation allows keyless auth from the pipeline.
AWS X-Ray / Cloud Trace / Azure Application Insights
— distributed tracing that spans the agent runtime, LLM API calls, and downstream tool calls with minimal instrumentation.
Teams wanting control without bare-metal ops
Security on cloud platforms follows the
: the provider secures physical infrastructure, hypervisor, and managed service internals; you secure the code, IAM configuration, network rules, and data handling.

— deploy Lambda or Cloud Run services inside a VPC so they reach your database over private IP rather than the public internet; use VPC endpoints for Bedrock, S3, and Secrets Manager to avoid internet traversal entirely.
— create a dedicated service account or execution role per agent. Grant access only to the specific resources that agent needs.
— use Secrets Manager's automatic rotation for database credentials; agents that cache secrets should re-fetch on a 401 response rather than assuming the cached secret is always valid.

— managed services (RDS Multi-AZ, DynamoDB Global Tables) provide high availability without custom code. For regional outages, a standby region with Route 53 health checks or Cloud DNS failover achieves RTO under 5 minutes.

— implement exponential back-off on LLM API calls and surface throttle errors as retryable events on the queue rather than hard failures, so no work is lost when a provider endpoint is temporarily unavailable.
Cloud platforms (AWS, GCP, Azure) provide managed compute, storage, queuing, and LLM APIs that eliminate most infrastructure operational work for AI agent teams.
Serverless runtimes (Lambda, Cloud Run, Container Apps) offer scale-to-zero billing that is economical for bursty agent workloads.
Co-locate agents with their LLM provider in the same region to reduce latency and simplify IAM-based authentication.
Inject secrets at runtime via Secrets Manager; never bake API keys into container images.
Budget LLM call time and overall invocation time explicitly — cloud execution time limits and LLM rate limits are both hard constraints.
Follow least-privilege IAM, VPC isolation, and structured logging from the start — retrofitting these controls is significantly harder than building them in.
The main trade-offs are vendor lock-in and cold-start latency; both are manageable through provider-agnostic LLM clients and provisioned concurrency.
